advantages: Internal metrics (inertia, silhouette) work without ground-truth labels, enabling evaluation in purely exploratory clustering.
Silhouette summarizes both cohesion and separation in a single bounded score interpretable per point and per cluster.
External metrics (ARI, NMI) provide objective benchmarks when reference labels exist for research or sanity checks.
Combining elbow, silhouette, and stability checks reduces over-reliance on any single heuristic for choosing k.
disadvantages: Inertia monotonically favors larger k and assumes spherical, equal-variance clusters—misleading for elongated or uneven-density groups.
Silhouette is distance-metric-dependent and computationally heavier than inertia; high scores do not guarantee business value.
External metrics require labeled data that may be incomplete, noisy, or not aligned with the clustering objective.
No single metric captures stability, interpretability, and downstream ROI—evaluation must stay multi-criteria.
